medications.indication_rationale: First-line treatment for symptomatic cutaneous lichen planus — reduces the inflammatory papules and the intense pruritus, helping break the itch-scratch cycle. Applied directly to the affected lesions.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Topical ointment applied to the affected wrist/ankle lesions; the source specifies nocturnal application under a clothing barrier. Exact strength and daily frequency are not stated in the source note and must not be invented — prescriber to set per Therapeutic Guidelines.
medications.duration: Short course with review at 7-10 days for efficacy (potent topical steroids are used time-limited on flexural skin).
contraindications_in_this_case: Apply only to non-infected skin — withhold or reassess if the excoriations become secondarily infected
Avoid prolonged potent-steroid use on thin flexural skin due to atrophy risk

medications.indication_rationale: A nocturnal sedating antihistamine relieves the night-time itch and aids sleep after two nights of deprivation, reducing unconscious scratching and secondary skin damage.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Oral, at night for nocturnal use as stated in the source; exact dose is not specified in the note and must not be invented — prescriber to set the dose.
medications.duration: Short-term, for the acute symptomatic period.
interactions_to_check: Additive sedation with alcohol or other CNS depressants
Anticholinergic burden — caution if used regularly in older adults

medications.indication_rationale: Regular emollient supports the damaged skin barrier and can reduce pruritus as an adjunct to the topical steroid.

Error of commission — treating the patient's feared 'contagious bug' as scabies with a scabicide is wrong: the morphology is lichenoid, there are no burrows, and empirical scabicide neither helps lichen planus nor is justified without evidence of infestation.

There is no established secondary infection (no heat, spreading erythema, purulence or fever); prescribing antibiotics now is unwarranted. They become appropriate only if the safety-net infection triggers are met.
Reserved for widespread or erosive/mucosal lichen planus; disproportionate and inappropriate for a mild, localised cutaneous presentation manageable with topical therapy.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep fingernails trimmed very short and apply the steroid ointment then cover the treated skin with long-sleeved cotton clothing before bed, creating a physical barrier against unconscious night-time scratching.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces self-inflicted excoriation while the steroid works overnight, protecting the skin barrier and lowering secondary-infection risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid washing the affected areas with hot water or harsh soaps; use lukewarm water and a gentle, soap-free cleanser.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Heat and detergents aggravate pruritus and further impair the barrier of already-inflamed skin.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Inspect the scratched areas daily for increasing redness, swelling, heat or discharge and monitor for fever.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Living alone, the patient is the only monitor for the principal complication (secondary infection); structured self-checking supports the safety-net.

patient_education_points.point: This is a localised autoimmune skin condition — the immune system is reacting against the top layer of skin. It is NOT contagious, not a parasite, and not an allergy.
The strong cream and the night-time antihistamine are to settle the inflammation and the itch so you can sleep; they do not cure it overnight.
The spots may take weeks to months to fade and often leave flat brown marks as they heal — this fading is expected, not a sign of harm.
Go back sooner (GP or, if unwell, urgent care/ED) if the scratched skin becomes hot, swollen, spreads redness or oozes yellow pus, or if you develop fevers or chills.
Tell your doctor if a similar rash or soreness appears inside your mouth or on the genitals, as that form needs different treatment and review.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response of the lesions and itch to the topical steroid, integrity of the excoriated skin, whether dermoscopy or a punch biopsy is warranted to confirm the diagnosis, and screening for any mucosal involvement.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Signs of secondary infection (hot, swollen, spreading or purulent skin, or fever), spread to mouth or genitals, or failure of the itch to improve with treatment.

must_include_items: Reach a presumptive benign inflammatory dermatosis and explicitly reassure the patient it is not contagious, not a parasite and not an allergy
Actively exclude scabies and secondary bacterial infection rather than anchoring on the patient's contagion fear
Provide symptomatic relief: a potent topical corticosteroid to the lesions plus a nocturnal sedating antihistamine for itch/sleep
Give skin-care and anti-scratch advice (short nails, cotton barrier at night, avoid hot water/harsh soaps)
Give an explicit secondary-infection safety-net with concrete triggers and where to go
Arrange GP review in about 7-10 days and flag that in-person dermoscopy/biopsy may be needed to confirm
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending an alternative potent topical corticosteroid (e.g. mometasone, methylprednisolone aceponate) instead of betamethasone dipropionate
Using an alternative sedating antihistamine at night in place of promethazine
Arranging the review via telehealth rather than in person if in-person dermoscopy is otherwise accessible
errors_of_omission: Not providing any itch relief, leaving the patient in the itch-scratch cycle
Failing to give a secondary-infection safety-net given the open excoriations and living alone
Not screening for mucosal involvement or a drug trigger
errors_of_commission: Treating as scabies with permethrin / empirical scabicide
Prescribing oral antibiotics with no signs of established infection
Prescribing systemic corticosteroids for mild localised disease
Over-triaging to the emergency department for a benign cutaneous condition
Asserting a confirmed diagnosis of lichen planus remotely without acknowledging telehealth limits
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) symptomatic itch relief, (b) a secondary-infection safety-net, and (c) avoidance of the scabicide/antibiotic/systemic-steroid over-treatment traps. Over-triage to ED or a confident remote diagnosis are scored failures.
